planting.note: Plant container or bare-root trees in spring in well-drained, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; golden rain tree is one of the few ornamental trees that blooms in summer (July–August) when most trees are not in bloom, and its papery seed pods provide additional ornamental interest through fall.

directions: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; golden rain tree is very adaptable to poor, dry, alkaline, and clay soils once established and requires minimal irrigation or fertilization. Koelreuteria paniculata is a Chinese ornamental tree 25–40 feet tall that fills a unique niche by blooming in midsummer (July–August) with spectacular 12–15 inch upright panicles of small, bright golden-yellow flowers followed by ornamental papery seed pods (lanterns) that are green, then pink-salmon, then tan, remaining decorative into winter; it is among the most summer-drought-tolerant and adaptable ornamental trees for zones 5–9. The compound leaves turn yellow in fall and the form is broadly rounded with age; plant with adequate space as the canopy spreads widely. Self-seeds freely in some regions — collect seed pods before they shatter to prevent volunteers.
sources: USDA Forest Service Urban Trees Guide

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container trees in spring or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; arguably the most floriferous crabapple species, with deep pink buds opening to white flowers in such abundance that the branches may not be visible at peak bloom.

directions: Plant in spring or fall in full sun in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Japanese flowering crabapple is among the most disease-resistant and reliably floriferous of all crabapple species, making it a lower-maintenance choice than many ornamental crabapple hybrids. Malus floribunda is a graceful, wide-spreading Japanese crabapple growing 15–25 feet tall with an equal or greater spread, famous for its spectacular spring display of deep rose-red to carmine buds that open to single white to pale pink flowers so profuse they completely cover the arching branches, followed by tiny (1/4 inch) red-and-yellow fruits that wildlife relish through fall and winter. It has outstanding resistance to apple scab, fire blight, and powdery mildew compared to many hybrid crabapples. Best used as a specimen tree where its graceful, weeping habit and spring floral display can be appreciated; needs full sun to bloom well.

planting.note: Start indoors in late summer (26–20 weeks before first frost) for a fall and winter harvest; January King is a savoy-type heirloom with semi-crinkled leaves that develop its characteristic red-purple outer coloring only after cold exposure — the heads taste best after frost has touched them.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before the transplanting date (late summer, aiming for outdoor planting in August–September for a winter harvest) or direct sow in early summer for fall maturity. January King is a centuries-old French and British heirloom winter cabbage that produces medium-sized, semi-round, savoy-type heads with beautifully crinkled, reddish-purple-flushed outer leaves and green-white inner leaves; it is exceptionally cold-hardy, standing in the garden through temperatures well below freezing and becoming sweeter with each frost. Transplant into well-amended, deeply prepared, slightly alkaline to neutral soil and firm the seedling in well to prevent wind-rocking; collar or brassica cage against caterpillars and aphids which are the main pests. In zone 8–9, grow as an overwintering crop planted in September for harvest January through March.

planting.note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; a widely adaptable Nantes-type hybrid carrot with outstanding disease resistance to leaf blight, powdery mildew, and cavity spot — one of the most reliable carrots for home gardens in hot and humid zones 8–11.

directions: Sow seed thinly, 1/4 inch deep, in well-loosened, stone-free soil 2–4 weeks before last frost; thin to 2–3 inches between plants when seedlings are 2 inches tall to allow roots to develop fully. Bolero is an outstanding Nantes-type hybrid carrot developed for exceptional disease resistance, particularly to alternaria leaf blight and carrot fly, with excellent tolerance to heat and humidity that makes it one of the most reliable carrots for gardens in zone 7–11 where summer heat normally makes carrot culture difficult. The 6–8 inch, cylindrical, blunt-tipped roots are uniformly deep orange, smooth-skinned, and have a sweet, mild flavor with good core-to-cortex ratio and excellent storage quality — they can be left in the ground for weeks without going woody. In zone 8–9, sow in late winter for spring harvest and again in late summer for fall harvest.

planting.note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost in loose, well-prepared soil; Yellowstone is one of the best yellow carrots, with a bright, true yellow exterior and interior and a distinctly sweet, mild, fruity flavor that stands out from standard orange carrots.

directions: Sow seed thinly 1/4 inch deep in deeply loosened, friable, stone-free soil 2–4 weeks before last frost; thin to 2–3 inches when seedlings reach 2 inches tall. Yellowstone is one of the top-performing yellow carrots for home gardens, producing uniform, 7–9 inch, Nantes-type cylindrical roots with vivid yellow skin and flesh throughout and a notably mild, sweet, slightly fruity flavor that some tasters prefer to standard orange carrots; it lacks the beta-carotene of orange types but is rich in lutein, a carotenoid beneficial to eye health. Excellent for fresh eating, roasting (where it holds its color beautifully), and for adding visual variety to rainbow carrot mixes. The flavor improves after mild frost. Direct sow works best as the taproot does not transplant well.
